skill.name: authorizing-api-requests
skill.description: Guides safe authentication and authorization for Mailtrap API requests, including token scopes, auth headers, account IDs, and secret storage.
skill.summary: Authorize Mailtrap API calls with correct tokens, headers, account IDs, and secret handling.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged command execution, network access, environment access, and credential patterns in SKILL.md. Review found a Markdown-only guidance file with Mailtrap API examples, no executable scripts, and no prompt injection attempt. The remaining risk is low because generated examples may make authenticated Mailtrap requests if a user supplies valid tokens.

low_findings.title: Authenticated Mailtrap API Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes curl examples that call Mailtrap endpoints with user-provided tokens. This is expected for an API authorization guide, but users should review generated requests before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly make network calls to Mailtrap endpoints using environment variable tokens. The behavior matches the skill purpose and does not show unauthorized exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution In Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documents a shell one-liner that derives MAILTRAP_ACCOUNT_ID from the Mailtrap Accounts API. This is not executed by the skill, but copied commands should be reviewed before use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution pattern is present in a fenced Markdown example. It calls an expected Mailtrap endpoint and does not include evidence of malicious command injection.

low_findings.title: Credential Handling Guidance Mentions Environment Files
low_findings.description: The skill discusses environment variables, .env files, CI secrets, and secret managers. This is defensive guidance, not code that reads local secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text instructs users not to paste or commit tokens and recommends secret stores. No file read, secret extraction, or credential transmission code exists in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Command Execution Matches Are Markdown Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Backticks and fenced shell snippets caused many command execution matches. They are documentation examples in SKILL.md and are not executable skill code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The only reviewed file is Markdown, and the flagged syntax appears inside tables, inline code, or fenced examples. No executable package, script, or handler is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Mailtrap Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Mailtrap documentation and API hosts. No evidence found of third-party exfiltration endpoints or unrelated network destinations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All visible network destinations are Mailtrap hosts or Mailtrap documentation links. This aligns with the skill description and no suspicious external domain appears.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Not Confirmed
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography alert appears to match authentication wording in the front matter. No cryptographic algorithm or implementation is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are a plain-language description of Mailtrap authorization use cases. They contain no hash, cipher, randomness, or cryptographic code.

content.value_statement: Mailtrap API calls fail when tokens, scopes, or account IDs are wrong. This skill gives clear rules for secure headers, token storage, and account resolution.
actual_capabilities: Explains which Mailtrap token scope to use for sending, sandbox, and account APIs.
Shows the accepted Authorization and Api-Token header formats.
Defines standard environment variable names for Mailtrap tokens and account IDs.
Guides users to resolve account_id from the Mailtrap Accounts API.
Lists common authorization mistakes and direct fixes.
limitations: Does not create or rotate Mailtrap API tokens for the user.
Does not verify live account permissions without a valid token.
Does not replace Mailtrap product documentation for every endpoint.
Does not prevent unsafe copying of generated shell commands.

use_cases.title: Prepare A Mailtrap Integration
use_cases.description: Choose the right token scope, header, and account_id before writing integration code.

use_cases.title: Secure CI Email Workflows
use_cases.description: Store Mailtrap tokens in CI secrets and expose them as environment variables.

use_cases.title: Debug Authorization Failures
use_cases.description: Check token scope, account access, and header format when Mailtrap returns 401 or 403.

prompt_templates.title: Pick The Right Token
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to call the Mailtrap API for [use case]. Tell me which token scope, header, and environment variables to use.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Auth Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Mailtrap API authentication plan for security issues. Focus on token storage, scopes, and account_id handling.

prompt_templates.title: Debug 401 Or 403
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me debug a Mailtrap 401 or 403 response. Ask for safe details and do not request my literal token.

prompt_templates.title: Design Multi-Environment Secrets
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Mailtrap token and account_id strategy for local, CI, staging, and production environments.

output_examples.input: I need to send production email through Mailtrap from a backend service.
output_examples.output: Use an Email Sending API token stored as MAILTRAP_API_TOKEN. Use the Bearer header and keep sandbox credentials separate.

output_examples.input: My contacts API request returns forbidden.
output_examples.output: Check that the token has account-level access and that MAILTRAP_ACCOUNT_ID matches the account the token can administer.

output_examples.input: Can I paste my Mailtrap token into this prompt for debugging?
output_examples.output: Do not paste the token. Describe the error, header type, scope, endpoint family, and where the token is stored.

best_practices: Use the narrowest Mailtrap token scope that supports the task.
Store tokens in environment variables or a secret manager, never in source code.
Keep sandbox and live sending credentials separate in every environment.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Mailtrap tokens or account IDs in generated code.
Do not pass tokens as command-line flags or paste them into prompts.
Do not use a live sending token against sandbox workflows.

faq.question: Which Mailtrap auth header should I use?
faq.answer: Use Authorization with a Bearer token for new code. Use Api-Token only for older clients that need it.

faq.question: Can this skill create a Mailtrap token?
faq.answer: No. It explains how to choose and store tokens, but token creation happens in Mailtrap settings.

faq.question: Should I store MAILTRAP_ACCOUNT_ID in code?
faq.answer: No. Store it as environment configuration or resolve it from the Accounts API for each environment.

faq.question: Does sandbox use the same token as live sending?
faq.answer: No. Use a separate sandbox token so testing cannot send real email by accident.

faq.question: Why did the audit report network risk?
faq.answer: The skill includes Mailtrap API examples. They are expected, but users should review commands before running them.

faq.question: Is it safe to paste a token into Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. Use the host secret store or environment variables and refer to placeholder names in prompts.
